coredata.dc:title: Poria cocos-derived carbon dots for parallel detection of Cr6+/Fe3+ in complex environments with superior sensitivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yuanping
Yuan, Song
Zhu, Minjie
Zhang, Zongwen
Cheng, Beijun
Xu, Wenjun
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional sensor capable of parallel sensing is of great importance thanks to their wide applications and great practicality. In this report, Poria cocos-derived carbon dots (CDs) were adopted for the development of multifunctional sensor for the parallel detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ with superior sensitivity and applicability. Specifically, extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.07 × 10−3 nM and 1.98 × 10−3 nM were achieved for Cr6+ and Fe3+, respectively. Systematic mechanism explorations revealed that the highly sensitive detection of Cr6+ was attributed to an efficient inner filter effect (IFE), while the sensing of Fe3+ was realized due to a strong static quenching process. Furthermore, the assay was found to be extremely versatile, achieving the reliable detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ in multiple natural water environments and even biological environment. Utilizing the different reactions of Cr6+ and Fe3+ towards masking reagents, a logic gate that could effectively eliminate the mutual interference of Cr6+ and Fe3+ was successfully designed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Parallel detection
Carbon dots
Cr6+
Fe3+
Poria cocos

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reliability and applicability of the assay
Differentiation of Cr6+ and Fe3+ and construction of logic gate

Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147). B. Chen acknowledges the financial support from the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation under the grant number: ZR2020QB040. Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the preparation of CDs from Poria cocos; (b) TEM image of CDs, the upper right inset showed a magnified view of a specific particle; (c) histogram of the size distribution of CDs; (d) The UV–vis absorption (black curve), fluorescent excitation (red curve) and emission (blue curve) spectra of aqueous dispersions of CDs at room temperature; insets showed photographs of aqueous dispersions of CDs under ambient light (left) and UV excitation (right), respectively; (e) emission spectra of aqueous dispersions of CDs under excitation of lights with varied wavelengths.

(a) XRD spectrum of CDs; (b) FTIR spectra of raw materials Poria cocos (black) and CDs (red); (c) XPS survey spectrum of CDs; (d) O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs; (e) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs; (f) C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs.

(a) Fluorescent intensity ratios F/F0 in the presence of CDs dispersions (blank) and 18 common metal ions (from left to right, Ag+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Al3+, Cr3+ and Cr6+), where F and F0 are the emission intensities of CDs at 445 nm in the presence and absence of the respective metal ions, as indicated in the figure; (b) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs in the presence of Cr6+ of different concentrations (0–300 μM), the emissions were excited at the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm and recorded at 445 nm; (c) relationship between Cr6+ concentrations and fluorescent intensities of CDs in the range of 0–300 μM; (d) the calibration curve for Cr6+ in the range of 0–130 μM; (e) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ of different concentrations (0–750 μM), the emissions were excited at the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm and recorded at 445 nm; (f) relationship between Fe3+ concentrations and fluorescent intensities of CDs in the range of 0–750 μM.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs only (black curve), Cr6+ solution (red curve), CDs in the presence of Cr6+ (blue curve) and the mathematical curve (green curve) obtained by subtracting the red curve (Cr6+ only) from the blue curve (CDs + Cr6+), respectively. The negative portion of the spectrum in the green curve of the mathematical calculation is not shown, as it has no physical meaning. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence (black) and presence of Cr6+ (red). (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr6+ (black curve), fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (red curve), and excitation spectrum of CDs (blue curve). (d) The observed (black dots, (IE)observed) and corrected (red dots, (IE)corrected) inhibition efficiencies after the addition of Cr6+ of different concentrations (0, 37.5, 75, 112.5, 150, 187.5, 225, 262.5 and 300 µM) to CDs dispersions, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratios after the addition of 1 mM amino acids (Arg: arginine; Lys: lysine; Cys: cysteine) to the CDs + Cr6+/Fe3+ system; (b) fluorescence intensity ratios after the addition of Arg to the CDs + Cr6+ system and the addition of Lys to the CDs + Fe3+ system, respectively; (c) the ratio of fluorescence intensity between CDs and solutions containing different ions; (d) fluorescence intensity ratios of pure CDs and with solutions containing different ions. F and F0 are the fluorescent emission intensities at 445 nm in the presence and absence of additives in the CDs dispersion, respectively.
Figure showing (a) the logic scheme of the “AND” logic gate, and (b) the truth table.
Results of Cr6+ recovery experiments performed in DI water, mineral water, tap water, lake water, and serum.

head.ce:title: Poria cocos-derived carbon dots for parallel detection of Cr6+/Fe3+ in complex environments with superior sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional sensor capable of parallel sensing is of great importance thanks to their wide applications and great practicality. In this report, Poria cocos-derived carbon dots (CDs) were adopted for the development of multifunctional sensor for the parallel detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ with superior sensitivity and applicability. Specifically, extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.07 × 10−3 nM and 1.98 × 10−3 nM were achieved for Cr6+ and Fe3+, respectively. Systematic mechanism explorations revealed that the highly sensitive detection of Cr6+ was attributed to an efficient inner filter effect (IFE), while the sensing of Fe3+ was realized due to a strong static quenching process. Furthermore, the assay was found to be extremely versatile, achieving the reliable detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ in multiple natural water environments and even biological environment. Utilizing the different reactions of Cr6+ and Fe3+ towards masking reagents, a logic gate that could effectively eliminate the mutual interference of Cr6+ and Fe3+ was successfully designed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of multifunctional sensors capable of detecting multiple target analytes simultaneously is of particular importance. Firstly, they could enable simultaneous monitoring of important analytes (i.e., Cr6+ and Fe3+), which helps to comprehensively assess the state of pollution in environment and living organisms. Secondly, they could also improve the efficiency of detection, which is conducive to the timely adoption of interventions. Lastly, they may even provide a scientific basis for the formulation of more effective environmental protection policies and public health strategies through real-time monitoring and data analysis. Among the various toxic metal ions, Cr6+ is of great concern because of its unique hazardous properties and wide presence in industrial wastewaters [1]. The high toxicity of Cr6+ is mainly due to its damaging effect on cell structure and function, which can trigger a series of biochemical reactions leading to cell damage and even death [2]. Cr6+ has excellent water solubility, which greatly enhances its ability to migrate in environmental media. The high mobility allows Cr6+ to easily spread to a wider area, not only contaminating water sources, but also entering organisms through the food chain, posing a potential threat to the ecosystem and human health [3]. Meanwhile, iron, as an important essential trace element, plays important roles in many biochemical pathways in humans and other organisms. It is involved in numerous important physiological functions such as oxygen transportation, energy metabolism and immune system regulation. The level of Fe3+ in body must be kept within an appropriate range to maintain normal physiological functions and metabolic homeostasis of the organism [4]. As such, the achievement of parallel detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ with one-single sensing system is scientifically interesting and practically meaningful.

In this context, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention thanks to their unique photoluminescent (PL) properties, low toxicity, high environmental benefits, and easy accessibility and affordability, which makes them ideal candidates for the development of multifunctional sensor [5–7]. Despite significant advancements, the field still faces several challenges to meet the full potential and expectations of multifunctional CDs sensors. Firstly, the sensing of multiple analytes by majority of current CDs systems is realized via sequential detection mode based on the “on-off-on” type of mechanism [8–10], in which the selective response to the latter analyte is exclusively depending on the prerequisite of successful detection of the first analyte. As such, it is difficult to selectively respond to subsequent analytes without specific preconditions, which limits their real-time monitoring performance in complex environments to a certain extent. Secondly, even a few systems realized the parallel detection of multiple analytes; the construction of these sensing systems usually involves complex operations and stringent conditions including modifications of CDs [11–13], adoption of multiple components [14–17], use of specific buffer solutions [18,19], regulation of an accurate pH and so on [14,20]. Clearly, these requirements undoubtedly increased the complexity and difficulty of their practical applications. As such, there is a relative lack of sensing systems based on unmodified, pristine CDs that could realize the effective, parallel detection of multiple metal ions (i.e., Cr6+ and Fe3+) [21–24].

The CDs were prepared by following our previously reported study [25] with slight modification. Specifically, 1 g of Poria cocos powder was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water and sonicated for 5 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h, after which the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to r.t. The resulted yellow aqueous dispersion was centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 5 min to remove any large particles, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a microporous filter membrane (0.22 μM). The final product was obtained as brown gel after freeze-drying.

To develop the calibration curve for the detection of Cr6+, CDs dispersion of 5000 μg/mL and Cr6+ solution of 22.5 mM were firstly prepared. Then the two were mixed in different ratios to make a series of dispersions in which the concentrations of CDs were kept as a constant (833 μg/mL), while those of Cr6+ were gradually increased (0, 9.75, 19.5, 37.5, 56.25, 75.00, 93.75, 112.5, 131.25, 150.00, 168.75, 187.50, 206.25, 225.00, 243.75, 262.50, 281.25 and 300.00 μM). The PL of the resulted dispersions were measured carefully after the dispersions were stored for 5 mins, and a calibration curve was constructed based on the PL intensities of CDs and concentrations of Cr6+. The calibration curve for the detection of Fe3+ was constructed following the exact procedures except that the gradient concentrations were slightly different (0, 18.75, 37.50, 75.00, 112.50, 150.00, 187.50, 225.00, 262.50, 300.00. 337.50, 375.00, 412.50, 450.00, 487.50, 525.00, 562.50, 600.00, 637.50, 675.00, 712.50 and 750.00 μM).

The LODs in the study were determined according to the well accepted equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of at least 20 blank samples and k is the slope of the linear calibration curve. To determine the σ value, 20 blank samples (aqueous dispersions of CDs without metal ions) were prepared and their PL spectra ( Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material) were measured with the same instrument under the same conditions of the analytes. The corresponding PL intensity values at 445 nm of these blank samples ( Table S3 in Supplementary Material) were then used to calculate the standard deviation σ.

To probe the specificity of this sensing assay, 16 common metal ions including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Al3+ and Cr3+ were selected as the interfering ions. Concentrations of all the metal ions were set at 750 μM and all experimental steps were similar to those discussed above.

To validate the reliability of the sensing assay, 19 spike solutions of Cr6+ and Fe3+ were prepared with DI water, mineral water, tap water, lake water and serum. Then the concentrations of these spike solutions were determined according to the established calibration curves and compared to their actual values. The recoveries were used to evaluate the reliability and calculated according to the following equation:
(1)

The validation of the IFE mechanism was verified by following the Parker’s equations below [26,27]:

where, CF stands for the correction factor; d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm); g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm) and s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm). Aex and Aem represent the absorbances at the excitation wavelength (λex = 360 nm) and the maximum emission wavelength (λem = 445 nm), respectively, of CDs.

where Fobserved is the measured PL intensity of CDs in the presence of Cr6+, Fcorrected is the actual PL intensity of CDs after excluding the influence of Cr6+ by IFE correction, and Fcorrected = CF × Fobserved [28,29]. (IE)observed is the inhibition efficiency in the presence of Cr6+ and (IE)corrected is the inhibition efficiency after excluding the effect of Cr6+ by IFE correction.

On-off-on strategy: Firstly, Cr6+ and Fe3+ solutions (375 μM) were pre-added to the aqueous dispersion of CDs, and the PL intensities of CDs after Cr6+/Fe3+ addition were measured and recorded. Then, 1 mM of the same amino acids (Arg: arginine; Lys: lysine; Cys: cysteine) were added to the Cr6+ and Fe3+ system, respectively, following which the PL intensities were measured and compared to those without amino acids addition.

Double masking strategy: Firstly, Cr6+ and Fe3+ solutions (375 μM) were pre-added to the aqueous dispersion of CDs, and the PL intensities of CDs after Cr6+/Fe3+ addition were measured and recorded. Then, 750 μM of BaCl2 solution was added to the Cr6+ system while 750 μM of NaF solution was added to the Fe3+ system. After the two were fully mixed, BaCl2 and NaF were able to effectively interact with Cr6+ and Fe3+ according to the following equations, which could reduce the quenching effect of Cr6+/Fe3+ on the CDs to achieve the double masking effect.
(6)

2 B a

(7)

]
3 -

The CDs reported in this study were synthesized from the extracts of Poria cocos powders via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment and the final product was obtained as brown gel (Fig. 1
a). The CDs were characterized by various methods to elucidate their morphological, structural, optical, and chemical properties. Firstly, the morphologies of the CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown, the CDs were well-dispersed spherical particles with no evident aggregation (Fig. 1 b); the diameters of these particles ranged from 1.5 to 5.5 nm, with an average of 3.4 nm (Fig. 1 c). Furthermore, the HRTEM image of the CDs (Fig. 1 b, inset) showed distinct lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) crystallographic plane of graphene, indicating the successful carbonization and graphitization of the carbon precursor [30,31].

Then, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed to further evaluate the optical properties of the sample. As shown, the aqueous dispersion of CDs was brownish-yellow under ambient light, and turned to blue-green upon excited by a portable light source of 400 nm (Fig. 1 d, insets). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs displayed broad absorption peaks around 200–300 nm, which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC (224 nm) and the n-π* transition of CO (287 nm) (Fig. 1 d, black curve) [32,33]. The dispersion subsequently exhibited an optimal excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 1 d, red curve) and a maximum emission at 445 nm (Fig. 1 d, blue curve). Specifically, the CDs demonstrated typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions, in which the emission peaks shifted from 442 to 492 nm as the excitation wavelengths gradually increased from 350 to 400 nm (Fig. 1 e). In addition, the absolute PLQY for CDs was determined to be 3.88 %.

The PL stabilities of CDs dispersions under different circumstances were also systematically investigated. Firstly, CDs dispersed in NaCl solutions of different concentrations (0–1000 mM) exhibited very similar PL intensities, highlighting the high resistance of CDs PL towards solutions of different ionic strength ( Fig. S2
a). Secondly, the CDs also demonstrated their excellent resistance to photobleaching under prolonged xenon light irradiation: our experiments showed that the fluorescent emission intensities of the CDs did not show significant attenuation even after 100 min of continuous illumination ( Fig. S2
b). Lastly, it was common that the PL of CDs could be heavily influenced in different pH environments [25]. Therefore, we also explored the effects of pH on the PL intensities of CDs. To our delight, it was clearly that pH did not exert significant influence on the PL intensities of the CDs in the pH range of 3–9 ( Fig. S2
c). Even under extremely acidic (i.e., pH < 2) or alkaline conditions (i.e., pH = 10), the CDs also kept acceptable PL stability. The excellent PL stabilities were seen as a favorable factor for the development of superior fluorescence sensing assays.

Following the optical properties, the structural and chemical characteristics of the CDs were also carefully investigated. It can be seen that the XRD pattern displayed a wide diffraction peak at 21.1° (Fig. 2
a), which is a common occurrence in CDs, and could be generally attributed to the crystalline graphitic structure of the carbon core [34]. The surface functional groups of CDs were then analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy, and both the CDs and their carbon precursor Poria cocos exhibited similar characteristic peaks from 500 to 4000 cm−1 (Fig. 2 b), which indicated that CDs inherited significant surface chemical moieties from their carbon predecessors [25]. Specifically, the absorption peaks at 3287 and 2897 cm−1 were attributable to OH/NH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. And the peaks at 1681, 1388 and 1011 cm−1 were assigned to CC, CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, which were comparable to those of the carbon precursor Poria cocos [35,36].

In addition, XPS provided further chemical characterizations of the CDs. In the XPS full spectrum, three peaks at 533.4, 399.5 and 285.7 eV could be observed, which corresponded to O 1s, N 1s and C 1s, respectively, and the atomic ratios of the O, N, and C were determined to be 41.3, 0.7, and 58.0 %, respectively (Fig. 2 c). As shown in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d), oxygen was present as CO (531.6 eV), CO/COH (532.4 eV) and OH (533.4 eV), respectively [30]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 e) could be split into two peaks corresponding to pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) [37] and graphitic N (401.4 eV). The high-resolution C 1s spectra could be fitted into four binding energies including 284.6, 286.1, 287.8, and 288.9 eV, which corresponded to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CN/CO, respectively (Fig. 2 f). Overall, the FTIR analysis and the XPS results were in good agreement.

Inspired by the existence of rich functional groups on the surface of CDs and prior studies showing that biomass-derived CDs exhibited selective fluorescence response to metal ions [38], we speculated that the CDs may also be utilized to detect metal ions. To test this hypothesis, different metal ions were added to aqueous dispersion of CDs to probe the specificity of CDs towards metal ions (Fig. 3
a). It could be seen that common monovalent ions (Green columns, i.e., Ag+, K+ and Na+), divalent ions (Blue columns, i.e., Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, and Fe2+) and trivalent metal ions (Purple columns, i.e., Al3+ and Cr3+) did not significantly reduce the PL intensity of CDs. However, to our delight, the addition of either Fe3+ (yellow column) or Cr6+ (red column) resulted in a significant decrease in the PL intensity of CDs, demonstrating the great potential of the CDs as sensing platforms for the specific detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reliability and applicability of the assay
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated above, the assay was highly sensitive towards Cr6+ and Fe3+, to further probe its sensing reliability, four spike Cr6+ solutions of varied concentrations in DI water were prepared and their calculated concentrations based on the aforementioned calibration curve were compared to their actual concentrations. It was obvious that the assay was reliable for all the spike solutions, attaining recoveries ranging from 95.3 % to 103.9 % (Table 1 , entries 1–4), all within the detection tolerance range. Among them, the recovery was extremely close to 100 % when the concentration of Cr6+ solution was 93.8 μM (Table 1, entry 1).

Similarly, reliability of the assay for the sensing of Fe3+ was also evaluated. The results showed that the recoveries were 100.6–104.1 % for DI water samples ( Table S4
, entries 1–4), 93.9–102.0 % for mineral water samples (entries 5–8), 98.6–108.8 % for tap water samples (entries 9–12), 94.4–105.4 % for lake water samples (entries 13–16) and 100.9–104.3 % for serum samples (entries 17–19). Clearly, all the samples were within the detection tolerance range and exhibited good recoveries, indicating that the method was also practical for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples and complex biological environment.

The quenching mechanisms of fluorophores are generally classified into three categories: static quenching, dynamic quenching and IFE. Static quenching takes place when the interaction of fluorophore and quencher results in the production of a non-fluorescent, stable ground-state complex, which would typically shift the UV–vis absorption of the fluorophore [31,41]. For dynamic quenching, high-energy electrons in the excited state could return to the ground state non-radiatively via energy or charge transfer due to the collisions between the quenching agent and the fluorophore. As a result, altered fluorophore lifetimes are generally expected in the presence of the quencher [5,42]. Unlike static or dynamic quenching, IFE could occur if absorptions of a quencher overlap well with either the excitation or emission spectrum of a fluorophore [5,43]. Thus, neither new UV–vis absorption peaks nor alteration of fluorophore lifetimes would be expected in a typical IFE quenching process [44].

To probe the quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were conducted. To begin with, the possible interaction between CDs and Cr6+ was analyzed base on their UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. It was clearly shown that the presence of Cr6+ did not have any obvious influence on the UV–vis absorptions of CDs (Fig. 4
a, black curve vs. green curve), indicating that the possibility of static quenching is very unlikely [42]. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs before and after the addition of Cr6+ were subsequently measured. The results showed that the average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs with and without Cr6+ were 2.79 and 2.83 ns (Fig. 4 b), respectively, which further ruled out the possibility of a dynamic quenching process [38].

The interaction of Fe3+ with CDs was also carefully analyzed with the same approaches. To begin with, a close comparison of the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in different chemical environments was carried out ( Fig. S9
a). To our surprise, the calculated UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ( Fig. S9
a, green curve) was quite different from that of CDs only ( Fig. S9
a, black curve). Specifically, the absorption centered at 282 nm is significantly decreased by 25 % ( Fig. S9
a), while a new absorption peak around 360 nm appeared ( Fig. S9
b). These features could serve as strong evidence that a static quenching process might have occurred between CDs and Fe3+. Furthermore, the average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs without and with Fe3+ were determined to be 2.71 and 3.08 ns ( Fig. S9
c), respectively, which could rule out the possibility of a significant dynamic quenching. Lastly, the Fe3+-quenched CDs PL could be facilely restored by the addition of amino acids (see Section 3.5), and amino acids were known to form stronger complexes with Fe3+ to destroy (or weak) the complexation of Fe3+ with CDs [46,47]. Collectively, we assumed that the quenching of CDs PL by Fe3+ was mainly through the pathway of static quenching [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of Cr6+ and Fe3+ and construction of logic gate
ce:section.ce:para:
Since Cr6+ and Fe3+ demonstrated similar PL quenching phenomena towards CDs, it was difficult to tell which ion was actually present simply based on the quenching assay. To address this problem, we developed two approaches to effectively differentiate the two ions. The first one was based on an “on-off-on” strategy that exploited the different quenching mechanisms of Cr6+ and Fe3+, which resulted different interaction towards amino acids [47]. Specifically, the Cr6+-quenched CDs PL demonstrated obvious declining while those of Fe3+-quenched exhibited clear recovery upon the addition of amino acids (Fig. 5
a). Based on the study, arginine (Arg) and lysine (Lys) were selected as the optimal amino acids for the differentiation. Specifically, in a Cr6+-quenched system, the PL of CDs is expected to be decreased by a factor of 3.6 with the addition of Arg, while that in a Fe3+-quenched system would be restored by a factor of 2.3 with the addition of Lys (Fig. 5 b).

The second approach was based on a double-masking strategy taking advantage of the different reactivity of Cr6+ and Fe3+ towards Ba2+ and F− (Eqs.
(6) & (7) ). Specifically, the Cr6+-quenched CDs PL could be facilely restored by a factor of 1.8 after the addition of Ba2+ (Fig. 5 c, green vs. yellowish green column), which could be attributed to the formation of insoluble BaCrO4 (Eq.
(6) ) releasing CDs from the Cr6+-CDs complex. Indeed, addition of Ba2+ to CDs-only dispersion did not exhibit such restoration effect; instead, the PL of CDs was slightly decreased (Fig. 5 c, red vs. orange column). Most importantly, this effect was unique to Cr6+, and could not be reproduced in a Fe3+-CDs system (Fig. 5 c, blue vs. purple column). On the other hand, the Fe3+-quenched CDs PL could be recovered by a factor of 1.8 after the addition of F− (Fig. 5 d, green vs. yellowish green column), which could be attributed to the formation of stable [FeF6]3− complex (Eq.
(7) ) attenuating the quenching effect of Fe3+ on CDs. Indeed, the above hypothesis was manifested by the fact that addition of F− to CDs-only dispersion did not result any enhancing effect (Fig. 5 d, red vs. orange column). Similarly, addition of F− to a Cr6+-CDs system did not result in any obvious PL alteration (Fig. 5 d, blue vs. purple column).

In view of the significant enhancement effects on CDs PL by the addition of Ba2+ and F− to the CDs-Cr6+/Fe3+ system, a molecular logic gate based on the “AND” logic operation was constructed (Fig. 6
a). In this design, Cr6+ and Ba2+, Fe3+ and F− were used as two independent input signals, and the degree of PL intensity recovery at 445 nm was used as the optical output response of the logic gate. Specifically, a significant recovery of PL intensity was defined as an “1” for the logic output, and conversely, if fluorescence was not recovered, it was represented as a “0” for the logic output (Fig. 6 b). For the definition of the input signals, the presence and absence of the four ions (Cr6+, Fe3+, Ba2+ and F−) were coded as “1” and “0”, respectively. When Cr6+/Ba2+ and (or) Fe3+/F− are co-present, the fluorescence signal could be significantly enhanced; in these cases, the logic gate would be activated to produce the logic output “1” signal, indicating that the “AND” logic function of the system was effectively realized.

Poria cocos-derived CDs with bright blue emission were facilely prepared by simple hydrothermal method, and found to be excellent sensing platform for the parallel detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+. Specifically, the assay for Cr6+ demonstrated good linearity in the range of 0–300 μM and the quenching of CDs PL was mainly via IFE mechanism, while the assay for Fe3+ exhibited good linearity in the range of 0–750 μM and the quenching was mainly attributed to static quenching. Regardless of the quenching mechanisms, both assays demonstrated extremely low LODs (1.07 × 10−3 nM for Cr6+ and 1.98 × 10−3 nM for Fe3+), leaving the assays among the most sensitive ones in literature. Furthermore, the assays have been successfully applied to the simultaneous detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ in DI water, natural water environments (i.e., mineral water, tap water and lake water) and biological sample (i.e., serum), all of which achieved good to excellent recoveries, highlighting the great potential of our assay towards Cr6+ and Fe3+ detection in complex environments. To effectively differentiate Cr6+ and Fe3+ in practical applications, two strategies including “on-off-on” and double masking were proposed, based on which, a logic gate that effectively eliminated the mutual interference of Cr6+ and Fe3+ were successfully constructed, thus realizing accurate identification and determination.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Z. Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147). B. Chen acknowledges the financial support from the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation under the grant number: ZR2020QB040. Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125346.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
